author: @totherarf
text:
A word of warning!
You need to be especially careful when welding galvanized steel! It produces very toxic gasses .... even more so than normal welding. It can literally send you mad .... also you get the same problem when forging the stuff!
Having said that I am sure that you have taken precautions ... this was really for those who do not know ;0)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I ground the zinc off mate as it doesn't weld that well either

replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering It was meant for those who follow and do not know ;0)

author: @tinkmarshino
text: To clunky for me.. you have to reach up inside to unhook the chain it in two places.. I think I might just take a piece of the pipe hinge it on the opposite side of the door and then make a simple  latching then attache one of those wires (or something stronger if needed) that swings loose but rests on the base of the door when not in use (or run it through the door so I wouldn't have to open it) so I can push the hinge open and bring it back to latch it.. Well I hope you can see my thought.. I have a hard time sometimes relaying what is in my mind to words on paper. Grand to have a cuppa with you in the am.. and bounce some ideas.. Carry on my friend.. well done!

author: @smob0
text: I've seen people make hooks similar to the ones you have made by cutting a chain link into a U or hook shape and welding in place. I would imagine it would be stronger then bent over stock because chain links typically go through an annealing and hardening process once they are shaped.

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, actually Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNAL) wrote that report for FEMA. Always thought that colander hanging by chain was hokey. Also the holes would need to be bigger on the colander. But is the gap between bars too big on your grate? I guess it depends on the biomass you choose to feed it. If you use pellets, then your gaps are too wide. Love your videos! I want to see this gasifier running soon! P.S. I thought it looked odd when the gasifer is hanging from the tractor that is running from the gasifier in that report.

author: @campbellspears3929
text: Short but sweet 👌

author: @waltersaunders7699
text: I know that your sin gas generator has yet to produce any gas for you Robert but, if the sin gas is suitably dry and clear of particulates, can it be compressed and stored for use at a later date?  If so, does it have a shelf life?

replies.author: @charlesdickens6706
replies.text:
......initially it's the wood volatiles that serve as fuel for very brief period only. No point compressing that bit. The real gaseous fuels are hydrogen and carbon monoxide in 50/50 molar ratio due to the essential reaction that is steam reacting with red hot carbon to yield these gases. It ain't like compressing LPG gasses which liquefy at moderate pressures. Aside from thus requiring at least 250 ATM pressures  to be worthwhile storage , the hydrogen component needs expensive technical solutions for high pressure storage . That's my understanding and ready to be corrected on a detail or two .
 The graphitic nitride Rob demonstrated presents a solution for hydrogen storage.
